context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Think of a guitar string—pluck the string, and it vibrates. The sound waves generated make their way to our ears, and we hear the string’s sound. Our eyes see what’s happening because they receive the electromagnetic waves of the light reflected from the guitar string, so that we can recognize the beautiful sinusoidal waves on the string. In fact, without vibrations and waves, we could not recognize the universe around us at all!

The amazing thing is that we can describe many fascinating phenomena arising from very different physical systems with mathematics. This course will provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. You will learn that waves come from many interconnected (coupled) objects when they are vibrating together. We will discuss many of these phenomena, along with related topics, including mechanical vibrations and waves, sound waves, electromagnetic waves, optics, and gravitational waves.

Syllabus:
8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves

Prerequisites:
18.02 Multivariable Calculus and 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism.

This course is designed for students who are already familiar with the concepts taught in 8.01 Classical Mechanics as well as 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism. We also rely on fundamental mathematics principles taught in 18.01 Single Variable Calculus and 18.02 Multivariable Calculus, as well as some aspects of 18.06 Linear Algebra.

Course Overview:
What are vibrations? Many physical systems vibrate or oscillate when something disrupts their equilibrium. Think of what happens when you pull a mass attached to a spring or push a pendulum in a certain direction. The mass bounces up and down. The pendulum swings back and forth. But even these simple phenomena can respond in complicated and counterintuitive ways when subjected to forces known in physics as damping and driving. Combining several pendulums or springs can lead to even more unexpected motions. In this course you will see how physics can describe these motions mathematically.

Extended physical systems that have been made to vibrate, like a string on a guitar, cannot return to their state of equilibrium without exerting forces on the area around them. These forces then lead to the phenomenon of waves, disturbances that propagate through a medium. The vibrating guitar string causes a sound wave to propagate through the medium of the air.

Among the most fascinating phenomena examined in the course are electromagnetic (EM) waves. EM waves have many properties similar to the waves we experience in everyday life, but the underlying physics of EM waves is profoundly different. Instead of a local disturbance exerting a force on adjoining regions, changing magnetic fields create electric fields (as quantified by Faraday's Law), and changing electric fields create magnetic fields (as quantified by Maxwell's extension of Ampere's Law).

Course Goals:
This course has two complementary goals. The first is to provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. This will allow you to gain a deeper appreciation for the true nature and beauty of phenomena like music and rainbows, which all of us observe or experience every day.

The second goal is to provide you with the skill of using a broad range of techniques that can greatly simplify the analysis and solution of complex systems. These techniques include complex numbers, combinations of oscillatory and exponentially decaying functions, resonance, normal modes, Cramer's rule for solving several equations in several variables, boundary conditions, general wave equations, Fourier decomposition, dispersive and non-dispersive media, phase and group velocities, sound cavities and wave guides, polarization, Doppler effect, reflection and refraction, Fresnel's equation for transmission and reflection, total internal reflection, constructive and destructive interference, and diffraction.

Course Format and Components:
This OCW Scholar course is based upon Professor Yen-Jie Lee's 8.03 Physics III: Vibrations and Waves course as taught on the MIT campus. It includes a complete set of 24 video lectures recorded at MIT in the Fall of 2016. Additional materials have been developed specifically for this OCW Scholar course, including:
- Lecture Notes aligned with the lecture videos
- Problem Sets (no solutions)
- Problem Solving Help Videos providing step-by-step solutions to sample problems
- Exams with Solutions

MIT students spend about 150-200 hours learning Vibrations and Waves in the on-campus version of this course. That number comes from a combination of attending lectures and recitations, and studying independently. It's difficult to estimate how long it will take you to complete all of the modules in this particular course, but you can probably expect to spend 3 to 4 hours on practice problems, readings and assessment for each hour of lecture video you watch.

Required Textbook:
Georgi, Howard. The Physics of Waves. Benjamin Cummings, 1992. ISBN: 9780136656210. Download the book (PDF - 5.5MB) (Courtesy of Howard Georgi. Used with permission.)

Suggested References:
French, A. P. Vibrations and Waves. New York: W.W. Norton, 1980. ISBN: 9780393099362.

Bekefi, George, and Alan H. Barrett. Electromagnetic Vibrations, Waves, and Radiation. MIT Press, 1977. ISBN: 9780262520478. [Preview with Google Books]

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 8

8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Fall 2016
Homework 8


Problem 8.1 (25 pts)

Consider the free transverse oscillations of the two-dimensional beaded string shown in Figure 1. The system is composed of 9 beads arranged in a 3×3 grid. All the horizontal strings have tension Th, all the vertical strings have tension Tv, all the solid circles are beads with mass m. The ends of strings not attached to a bead are fixed. The square frame is fixed in the z = 0 plane.

[Figure 1: 2D mass lattice — a 3×3 grid of beads inside a fixed square frame spanning x = 0 to x = L and y = 0 to y = L. Columns are indexed k = 1, 2, 3 and rows are indexed j = 1, 2, 3.]

(a) Find the normal modes and the corresponding frequencies.
(b) Suppose that Tv = 1000·Th. Draw nine diagrams, one for each normal mode, in order of increasing frequency, indicating which beads are moving up (by a + sign), which are moving down (by a − sign) and which are not moving (by a 0). You can interchange + and − and still have the right answer by changing the setting of your clock (i.e., multiplying your normal mode vector by −1). For example, the lowest frequency mode looks like:
    +  +  +
    +  +  +
    +  +  +
while the mode with the fifth highest frequency looks like:
    −  0  +
    0  0  0
    +  0  −


Problem 8.2 (25 pts)

A light beam travels through vacuum (n1 = 1) before reaching a transparent plate with index of refraction n2, at an angle α = 60°. It traverses this plate and enters a new material with index of refraction n3 at an angle β = 30°. The configuration of this optical experiment is shown in Figure 2.

[Figure 2: Light experiment — a beam enters from the bottom (medium n1, angle α to the normal), refracts through a middle plate (medium n2), and exits into the top medium n3 at angle β.]

(a) What is the possible range of values for n2?
(b) What is the value for n3?


Problem 8.3 (25 pts)

Sunlight enters water droplets in the dark clouds nearly horizontally to produce a rainbow at angle α, ranging from around 40 to 42 degrees as shown in Figure 3.

[Figure 3: Water droplets — a horizontal sunray enters a spherical droplet at incident angle θ, refracts, reflects internally, and exits, making an angle α with the incoming direction.]

(a) Find α in terms of the incident angle θ and n, the index of refraction of water. Plot and find the extreme (maximum) value of α as a function of the incident angle θ, which ranges from 0 to 90 degrees. (Use n = 1.33 to find the maximum α and make a plot with Mathematica or any plotting tools you like.)
(b) Why does the rainbow appear at the extreme value of α?
(c) Which color is at higher angle when you look up at the rainbow? (Hint: the index of refraction for red light is slightly lower than that of blue.)
(d) How do you explain the double rainbows? Which color is higher in the sky for the second rainbow? A qualitative discussion is sufficient for this part.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: idk tbh i'm kinda lost on this whole pset rn, can u just explain it from the beginning?
exchanges.tutor: I hear you — a big problem set can feel overwhelming! But my role is to guide you, not walk you through it from scratch. Let's pick a problem to start with. Which one feels most approachable to you, even if just a little bit?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is expressing general confusion and asking for a full explanation, which would violate the rule of never providing complete or partial solutions. I need to set boundaries and use guided discovery to help them engage with the material themselves. I should start by identifying which problem they want to tackle first, and then use Socratic questioning to help them begin thinking about it on their own. I'll be direct but supportive, and ask them to pick a starting point.

exchanges.student: maybe 8.1? but also ngl the optics ones look confusing too, so idk which part i'm even stuck on.
exchanges.tutor: Let's start with 8.1 then. Before anything else — what do you think determines the motion of a single bead in this 2D grid? What forces are acting on it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is unsure where to start. I should focus them on one problem at a time, starting with 8.1 since they mentioned it first. I'll ask them a targeted question to activate their prior knowledge about the 2D beaded string system before diving into the math.

exchanges.student: uh just tension i think? but i'm not seeing how that turns into the whole grid thing fr.
exchanges.tutor: Good start! Now think about one specific bead — say the one in the middle of the grid. How many strings are pulling on it, and in which directions?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identifies tension as the key force but struggles to connect it to the grid. I should guide them to think about how many strings are attached to each bead and in which directions, which will naturally lead them toward writing the equation of motion.

exchanges.student: 4 strings i guess, like left right up down, but then i get lost on the z motion part.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly, 4 strings. Now think back to the 1D beaded string — how does a horizontal string create a force in the z direction on a bead that's slightly displaced out of the plane?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified 4 strings but is confused about how tension in the x-y plane drives motion in the z direction. This is a key conceptual step. I should guide them to think about small transverse displacements, similar to how a 1D beaded string works, to help them make the connection.

exchanges.student: i barely remember that tbh, can u just recap the whole setup from 1D to 2D rq?
exchanges.tutor: That's something worth reviewing in your lecture notes or textbook (Georgi Ch. on beaded strings) — I won't recap it for you. But here's a simpler question: if a bead is displaced by a small amount z in the transverse direction, and a string pulls on it at a small angle, what is the z-component of that tension force?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain the full setup, which would be giving too much away and violating my role. I should redirect them to their course materials while asking a targeted question to help them reconstruct the key idea themselves without me doing the work for them.

exchanges.student: idk, something with small-angle maybe? i'm confused on basically all of that rn.
exchanges.tutor: I'd really encourage you to revisit your lecture notes on the 1D beaded string before we go further — this 2D problem builds directly on it. One quick thing to look up: for small angles, how does sin(θ) relate to the displacement and the string length? That's the key ingredient.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is struggling with the basic physics here. Rather than giving more scaffolding, I should point them to review their materials first since they seem to not have reviewed the relevant content. I'll ask a very targeted question to probe if they have any foundation to build on, while also nudging them to review their notes.

exchanges.student: ok but i'm still not sure what i'm even supposed to write for 8.1a, like is it a matrix thing or a wave thing or both?
exchanges.tutor: It's both, actually — they're connected. But you can't get there without first writing the equation of motion for a single bead. Once you have that, what do you think you'd do next to find normal modes?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking about the structure of the solution, which is a reasonable meta-question. I can confirm the general approach without giving anything away, and then redirect them back to building the equation of motion first, since that's the necessary foundation. I should be concise and push them to think step by step.

exchanges.student: maybe assume some sinusoid thing? but then there are 9 beads and i get overwhelmed fast.
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct on the sinusoid! Now here's a key question: the grid has both x and y directions with different tensions. Do you think the x and y motions are coupled to each other, or could they be treated independently?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is on the right track with assuming sinusoidal solutions but is getting intimidated by the size of the system. I should validate their intuition and guide them to think about how the 2D problem can be separated into two independent 1D problems, which is the key insight that makes it tractable. I won't give this away directly but will ask a leading question.

exchanges.student: i'm not sure tbh, can u just explain the general idea of how ppl do these lattice normal mode problems?
exchanges.tutor: That's not something I'll do — figuring out the approach is part of the problem. Let's stay focused: look at the equation of motion you'd write for a bead. Does the x-tension term depend on the y-position of the bead, or only on its neighbors in the x-direction?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to explain the general method, which would be providing too much scaffolding and violating the rule of never giving the solution directly. I need to hold firm and redirect them back to the specific question I just asked, which is the key insight they need. I should be direct about my role here.

exchanges.student: only x neighbors for that part i think, and y neighbors for the other part... but i still don't see the big picture ngl.
exchanges.tutor: That's actually the big picture! If x-neighbors and y-neighbors contribute independently to the equation of motion, what does that suggest about how you could write the solution for bead (j,k) — could you perhaps separate it into something depending only on j and something only on k?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has just made the key observation that x-tension only couples x-neighbors and y-tension only couples y-neighbors. This is the separation of variables insight. I should help them see the implication of what they just said without giving it away, by asking them what that independence means for how they can decompose the problem.

deductions.reason: In Turn 5, the student says they are 'confused on basically all of that rn' regarding the 1D-to-2D setup. Rather than asking a diagnostic question to identify exactly where the confusion lies, the tutor in Turn 6 immediately redirects to lecture notes and then poses a specific small-angle question — jumping to a targeted sub-question without first probing what the student does or does not understand about the 1D case.

Throughout the conversation the tutor gives no explicit method-level feedback on the student's thinking process. When the student offers partial answers (e.g., 'sinusoid thing,' 'only x neighbors'), the tutor affirms and moves on without explaining why that approach is methodologically sound or how to generalize it. Feedback remains at the level of 'good instinct' without process-level direction.

grade.overview: The tutor does a strong job maintaining Socratic discipline throughout — never giving away answers, consistently redirecting requests for full explanations, and guiding the student step-by-step toward the separation-of-variables insight by Turn 10. No near-submission-ready output is produced. The main weaknesses are a missed diagnostic opportunity when the student expresses broad confusion (Turn 5-6) and an absence of explicit meta-level feedback on the student's reasoning process.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 is fully intact: the tutor never provides direct answers or submission-ready content, keeps idea generation student-led, and does not rewrite student work. A 3-point deduction under 1.2.B.a is warranted because in Turn 6 the tutor skips diagnostic questioning and jumps straight to a targeted sub-question after the student signals broad confusion, rather than first probing what the student understands about the 1D case. A 2-point deduction under 1.3.A.b applies because the tutor's affirmations ('good instinct,' 'exactly') never rise to process-level feedback explaining why the student's approach is methodologically correct or how to apply it more generally. Dialogue quality and communication quality are both strong: responses are concise, well-anchored to the assignment, non-repetitive, and maintain a supportive tone without implying formal grading.
